The mixed use core of the activity centre. Retail and commercial uses are
envisaged on the ground floor, with residential and office uses on the
upper floors.

Provides a transition area between the core of the activity centre and
surrounding medium density residential areas. Residential and compatible
commercial uses are promoted. Unsuitable commercial uses such as
Shops, Restaurant/Cafes, Liquor Stores, Small Bars and Taverns are not
permitted in this zone as per the LPS 6 Zoning Table.

Provides for medium density residential close to the activity centre and the
Canning Highway public transport corridor.
Provides for local and district recreational and sporting uses.

Activity centres should have a
diverse range of land uses rather
than one overly dominant use
(e.g. retail in shopping centres).
Shop/retail floorspace currently
occupies approximately 39% of
the total non-residential
floorspace. It is estimated that this
percentage may increase to 45%
in 2031 if the Melville Plaza
Shopping Centre is redeveloped.
The SPP 4.2 mix of land uses
target of 20% is well exceeded,
with 61% of non-residential
floorspace currently occupied by
non-shop/retail uses. This is
expected to drop to 55% in 2031,
which is still well above the SPP
4.2 target.

The mixed use core of the activity centre. Retail and commercial
uses are envisaged on the ground floor, with residential and office
uses on the upper floors.

Provides a transition area between the core of the activity centre
and surrounding medium density residential areas. Residential and
compatible commercial uses are promoted. Unsuitable commercial
uses such as Shops, Restaurant/Cafes, Liquor Stores, Small Bars
and Taverns are not permitted in this zone as per the LPS 6 Zoning
Table.

Provides for medium density residential close to the Canning
Highway public transport corridor.
Provides for local and district recreational and sporting uses.

LeisureFit Melville and A.H. Bracks
Library

Gym and swimming pool at Melville
Plaza

It is estimated that there is around
13,630m² of commercial floorspace (not
including health, recreation or culture)
currently and that this could grow by
around 50% by 2031 depending on market
demand.

Estimated area and percentage
of public open space given over
to:
 Regional open space
 District open space
 Neighbourhood parks
 Local parks

The LeisureFit Melville site is part of an
existing District level open space
(approximately 7.2 hectares).
There are no new regional, district or
neighbourhood parks proposed given the
small area of the activity centre. However
new small parks and public spaces could
be created under the plan.

Activity centres should have a diverse
range of land uses rather than one overly
dominant use (e.g. retail in shopping
centres).
Shop/retail floorspace currently occupies
approximately 39% of the total non-
residential floorspace. It is estimated that
this percentage may increase to 45% in
2031 if the Melville Plaza Shopping Centre
is redeveloped.
The SPP 4.2 mix of land uses target of
20% is well exceeded, with 61% of non-
residential floorspace currently occupied
by non-shop/retail uses. This is expected
to drop to 55% in 2031, which is still well
above the SPP 4.2 target.

Mixed Use – covers the Centre C2 and
Mixed Use zones, where a mix of land
uses is permitted.
The Residential zone is a predominately
residential area.
Public Open Space covers the area
reserved for open space.
Road Reserves cover 24% of the activity
centre and are therefore worth noting as a
major land use.

The subject area is an existing urban
activity centre rather than a greenfields
site to be subdivided.

The activity centre is planned to contribute
354 additional dwellings by 2031. The total
target across the City of Melville is 11,000
dwellings by 2031. The actual number of
new dwellings delivered will be based on
market demand.

31 dwellings per ‘gross’
hectare
41 dwellings per ‘net’
hectare

SPP 4.2 lists a desirable target of 30
dwellings per ‘gross hectare’ (the area
zoned ‘Urban’ under the MRS) for District
Centres. In this case, the calculation
removes the Canning Highway Primary
Regional Road Reservation from the total
site area. The plan aims to achieve 31
dwellings per gross hectare by 2031,
which slightly exceeds the desirable target.
‘Net hectare’ has been defined as the
activity centre area minus public open
space and existing road reserves as no
dwellings will be built on these reserves.
The remaining area could potentially be
developed with residential. The net
dwelling target is 41 dwellings per hectare.

This estimate is based on 550 total
dwellings with 2.3 people per dwelling,
which is the City-wide average number of
residents per dwelling
This is an existing urban area

Increase residential
density, intensity of
land-close to local
businesses,
employment, education,
cultural and recreational
opportunities, and
frequent public
transport.

 Locate higher residential densities near activity centres and
along public transport routes to maximise access and
convenience to services.
 Provide mixed density residential development within walking
distance of public areas including open space and key
destinations, such as shops, schools and medical centres and
accessible public transport.
 Mixed density developments should be integrated with
surrounding development, public transport and with supporting
infrastructure including walkways, public areas and cycle paths.

Improve accessibility,
integrate facilities and
have multiple uses of
space.

 Provide a range of development types and densities that provide
a mix of day and night time activities.
 Integrate new development with existing developments and
surrounding transport networks.
 Pay attention to lighting, street furniture, signage, footpath
treatment and safe road crossings to ensure a safe and
convivial space is provided for all users.
 Provide open space and recreation areas especially for children
and their carers.

Manage vehicle
traffic to provide
safe environments
for walking, cycling
and other physical
activities.

 Prioritise pedestrians, cyclists and public transport above car
use in transport policy.
 Design streets that promote vehicle speeds of no more than
30km per hour in residential streets, strip shopping and around
major trip generators (e.g. schools).
 Provide high quality pedestrian and bicycle facilities to service
major trip generation locations such as schools, hospitals and
shopping precincts.
 Encourage on-street car parking to calm traffic speeds, support
retail and commercial businesses and provide a buffer between
pedestrians and roads.
 Provide street crossings on busy streets and in the vicinity of
major trip generators including schools, hospitals, shops, parks
and public transport stops.

Streets and development on private land should be designed to
adapt to and mitigate anticipated climate impacts such as more
frequent heat waves and high intensity rainfall events and a hotter
climate.
Adaptation measures could include: climate sensitive design
(awnings, sun shades, eaves, breezeways, rooftop gardens or
vertical landscaping), water sensitive urban design, utilising double
(or triple) glazing, minimising paved surfaces and providing mature
trees with large canopies to cool the local micro-climate.
Mitigation measures could include: planting trees on-site or off-site to
absorb carbon, using energy efficiently through green building
design, purchasing renewable energy from a supplier, installing
renewable energy on-site (e.g. solar) or sustainable transport
measures (e.g. Travelsmart).

Well located, mixed use, higher density activity centres serviced by
frequent public transport are a much more sustainable urban form
than car-based urban sprawl and reduce resource consumption
(particularly the land required to accommodate the city’s population).

Living local allows people to access many daily needs within walking
or cycling distance of their home. This can reduce traffic congestion,
promote exercise, support local services and businesses and
encourage a sense of community. Mixed use activity centres help
support living local.

Walking is the most sustainable and cost-effective transport method
as it uses no resources and creates no environmental impacts.
Focussing on pedestrians first helps produce more people-friendly
places and better quality urban design.

Water sensitive urban design treats water as a valuable resource
rather than a waste to be disposed of. It encourages water runoff to
be utilised in gardens, water to be used efficiently and waste water to
be used on site where possible.

Upgraded streetscapes, high quality landscaping on private land,
rooftop gardens and vegetated walls will help create a greener and
cooler activity centre and reduce the urban heat island affect.
